coredata.dc:title: Preparation of concentration dependent nitrogen sulfur Co-doped graphene quantum dots by UV irradiation and reversible optical switch of hydroxypropyl methyl cellulose
coredata.dc:creator: Tang, Siyuan
Chen, Da
Wang, Changxing
Yang, Yongsheng
Li, Xiameng
Li, Tingting
Zhang, Xiaohong
coredata.dc:description: Nitrogen and sulfur co-doped graphene quantum dots (N, S-GQDs) are prepared by using o-phenylendiamine and catechol as raw materials under UV irradiation in dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO) solution for 2 h. The resultant N, S-GQDs show uniform size, good crystallinity and fluorescence stability. The tunable polychromatic luminescence is obtained by adjusting the concentration of N, S-GQDs. Then, N, S-GQDs/HPMC composite material is designed and synthesized, which exhibits light responsive optical switching performance owing to micro-aggregation efficacy of N, S-GQDs to transform light into heat and drive the phase transition of hydroxypropyl methyl cellulose (HPMC). Therefore, the prepared N, S-GQDs/HPMC with excellent solar modulation ability can easily switch between opaque and transparent states depending on weather condition. In the end, the thermoresponsive property of N, S-GQDs/HPMC endows it with good performance as functional materials matrix.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene quantum dots
Concentration effect
Photothermal conversion
Light-stimuli response
Optical switching

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: The concentration deployment of N, S-GQDs
Synthesis of N, S-GQDs modified hydroxypropyl methyl cellulose
Concentration-dependent emission behavior of N, S-GQDs
Light response and reversible light switch of N, S-GQDs/HPMC
Light intensity sensing based on the temperature variation of N, S-GQDs/HPMC

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: K C Wong Magna Fund in Ningbo University

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by projects from National Natural Science Foundation of China under Grant (Nos. 11704204 , 61604084 , and 51802337 ). K. C. Wong Magna Fund in Ningbo University . The authors declare no competing ﬁnancial interest.

This work was supported by projects from National Natural Science Foundation of China under Grant (Nos. 11704204, 61604084, and 51802337). K. C. Wong Magna Fund in Ningbo University. The authors declare no competing ﬁnancial interest.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM images of N, S-GQDs. (b) The corresponding particle size distribution. (c) AFM image and the height distribution of the N, S-GQDs. (d) Raman and (e) XPS spectrum of N, S-GQDs. Well-fitted high-resolution (f) C 1s, (g) N 1s, (h) O 1s and (i) S 2p spectra of N, S-GQDs.

(a) UV–vis absorption, PL excitation and PL emission spectra of N, S-GQDs. The inset is photograph of N, S-GQDs under daylight and 365 nm UV light. (b) PL emission spectra of N, S-GQDs solution at different excitation wavelengths. (c) Excitation-emission matrix of the N, S-GQDs. PL intensity of N, S-GQDs under different (d) irradiation time (365 nm), (e) storage time in daylight and (f) concentration of NaCl.

(a) Normalized PL emission spectra, (b) CIE coordinates and (c) UV–vis absorption spectra of N, S-GQDs dispersion with different concentrations. (d) Fluorescence lifetime spectra of N, S-GQDs solutions with different concentrations. (e) Schematic illustration of the possible energy level diagram of N, S-GQDs with the increase of concentration.

(a) Transmittance spectra of N, S-GQDs/HPMC with different irradiation time (100 mW/cm2). (b) Temperature variation and (c) the corresponding daylight and infrared images of HPMC and N, S-GQDs/HPMC solutions under different irradiation time. (d) Absorption spectra of HPMC, N, S-GQDs and N, S-GQDs/HPMC solutions. (e) Daylight and infrared images of the composites with different N, S-GQDs concentrations. (f) Schematic diagram of reversible light response and photothermal conversion mechanism of N, S-GQDs/HPMC.

(a) Schematic diagram of the fabrication of planar microcavity device based on N, S-GQDs/HPMC. (b) The temperature variation of N, S-GQDs/HPMC under different light intensities (Inset: the corresponding daylight and infrared images of planar microcavity). (c) The temperature variations of N, S-GQDs/HPMC under light on and light off conditions. (d) The reversible light-dependent of the N, S-GQDs/HPMC.
Schematic illustration for synthesizing N, S-GQDs by UV-exposure, and the concentration-relevant properties (left) and the photothermal conversion process (right).

head.ce:title: Preparation of concentration dependent nitrogen sulfur Co-doped graphene quantum dots by UV irradiation and reversible optical switch of hydroxypropyl methyl cellulose

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Microelectronic Science and Engineering, School of Physical Science and Technology, Ningbo University, Ningbo, 315211, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Microelectronic Science and Engineering
School of Physical Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Preventative Medicine and Zhejiang Provincial Key Laboratory of Pathological and Physiological Technology, School of Medicine, Ningbo University, Ningbo, Zhejiang, 315211, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Institute of Preventative Medicine and Zhejiang Provincial Key Laboratory of Pathological and Physiological Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Institute of Preventative Medicine and Zhejiang Provincial Key Laboratory of Pathological and Physiological Technology, School of Medicine, Ningbo University, Ningbo, Zhejiang 315211, China.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Nitrogen and sulfur co-doped graphene quantum dots (N, S-GQDs) are prepared by using o-phenylendiamine and catechol as raw materials under UV irradiation in dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO) solution for 2 h. The resultant N, S-GQDs show uniform size, good crystallinity and fluorescence stability. The tunable polychromatic luminescence is obtained by adjusting the concentration of N, S-GQDs. Then, N, S-GQDs/HPMC composite material is designed and synthesized, which exhibits light responsive optical switching performance owing to micro-aggregation efficacy of N, S-GQDs to transform light into heat and drive the phase transition of hydroxypropyl methyl cellulose (HPMC). Therefore, the prepared N, S-GQDs/HPMC with excellent solar modulation ability can easily switch between opaque and transparent states depending on weather condition. In the end, the thermoresponsive property of N, S-GQDs/HPMC endows it with good performance as functional materials matrix.

ce:section.ce:para: The development of photochromic or hydrogen storage materials to reduce energy consumption and environmental pollution is one of the most concerned topics [1–8]. Because of its transparency or color switchability, color rendering materials are regarded as an effectively and environmentally friendly technology [9,10]. At present, the preparation of thermochromic materials that respond to changes in the intensity of sunlight has become a fascinating area. For example: (i) a commercial thermochromic pigment which can change from opaque to translucent at about 48 °C [11], (ii) a commonly available saturated hydrocarbon converts from opaque to transparent at 40 °C [12] and (iii) a thermoresponsive polymer, such as hydroxypropyl methyl cellulose (HPMC) changes from transparent to opaque at their phase change temperature (45 °C) [13,14]. However, there is a limitation in the applications of thermochromic materials that their phase transition temperature is usually higher than the ambient temperature, which blocks their switch in the color or transparency in response to ambient temperature changes. It is of significance to reduce the phase transition temperature of chromic materials through design and synthesis of composite materials with heat-absorbing cores or the polymer composition and structure [15,16]. In both initiatives, the composite materials like nanoparticles, carbon dots and graphene oxide are considered as an effective way to change the color or transparency of thermochromic materials [17–19]. However, the low photothermal conversion efficiency, complex synthesis process and low stability are still an embarrassing obstacle for their widespread applications. Therefore, it is highly desired to develop new composite materials with high photothermal conversion efficiency, more stable and fast synthesis process.

As a zero-dimensional carbon nanomaterial with a size less than 10 nm, graphene quantum dots (GQDs) have some unique properties including adjustable optical properties, good water dispersion, good biocompatibility, low toxicity, rich carbon sources, etc. [20–23] In addition, the GQDs are popular in the fields of optical modulation, photocatalysis and energy conversion owing to their rich surface states, better physical and chemical properties [24,25]. Recently, GQDs-based polymers are getting more attractive in solar energy utilization, in which GQDs composites exhibit excellent photothermal or photoelectric conversion properties [26–28]. For instance, Briscoe and co-workers assembled all-solid-state solar cells by using carbon quantum dots which employed biomass as a carbon source by the means of hydrothermal treatment [29]. Ke et al. successfully prepared carbon quantum dots modified TiO2 photocatylyst by a facile sol-gel method, and achieved the photocatalytic degradation of methylene blue [30]. Unfortunately, the most of GQDs materials show the blue emission with low photoluminescence quantum yield, which greatly restricted the application of GQDs [2,31]. Moreover, the present synthesis processes are relatively complex and time-consuming. Therefore, it is important and attractive to develop a simple and rapid method to prepare GQDs and adjust their photoluminescence.

In the present study, the nitrogen and sulfur co-doped graphene quantum dots (N, S-GQDs) are prepared by using o-phenylendiamine and catechol as raw materials under UV irradiation in dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO) solution for 2 h. The prepared N, S-GQDs have uniform size, good crystallinity and high concentration of nitrogen and sulfur doping. By adjusting the concentration of N, S-GQDs solution, a tunable emission wavelength is realized from 505 nm (cyan) to 560 nm (yellow). In addition, N, S-GQDs are introduced into hydroxypropyl methyl cellulose (HPMC) to obtain N, S-GQDs/HPMC composites at room temperature. The obtained N, S-GQDs/HPMC composite material has strong sensitivity and reversibility to light, which can switch the transparent state under the influence of ambient temperature, and showing excellent solar light modulation ability. Therefore, it is of great significance to apply N, S-GQDs in the field of the thermal responsive functional materials.

Catechol (98%), o-phenylendiamine (98%), dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO), ethanol, hydroxypropyl methyl cellulose (HPMC), and other chemicals were purchased from Aladdin (Shanghai, China) and all chemical reagents were of analytical grade and utilized as obtained without further purification. Ultrapure water (Milli-Q water) was used during the experiments.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) (Model JEM-2100F, 200 kV, Japan Electron Optics Laboratory Ltd.) and atomic force microscope (AFM) (Oxford Instruments, Cypher S) were utilized to determine the morphology and size distribution of the N, S-GQDs. Raman spectrum was measured by HORIBA Jobin Yvon HR800 Evolution. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA) was used to determine the surface chemical composition and functional groups of the N, S-GQDs. The fluorescence excitation and emission spectra, the UV–vis absorption spectrum, as well as the quantum yield were measured with a multifunction fluorescence spectrophotometer (PerkinElmer LS55) from PerkinElmer Instruments (U.K.) at room temperature. The transmission spectra of the samples were recorded by UV–vis–NIR spectrophotometer (UV-3600 Plus, Shimadzu). The simulated solar illumination was provided by a 400 W xenon lamp (Beijing Au-light Co., Ltd.). The light intensity was controlled by adjusting the power of the light source. The digital photographs were taken with iPhone 12 (Apple Inc.). The temperatures and infrared thermal images of the samples were recorded by an infrared (IR) thermal camera (Fluke TiS75) with a measurement accuracy of ±0.1 °C and a thermocouple thermometer.

The quantum yield of the N, S-GQDs is tested as the previous report [32]. Specially, Rhodamine 6G (QY = 95% in ethanol at 420 nm) is selected as the standard sample. A series of N, S-GQDs solutions with different concentration are prepared to ensure all corresponding optical absorbance values are within 0–0.1 at room temperature. The following equation is used to calculate the quantum yields:
Q Y =
Q
S T
×
(
K X
K
S T
)
×
(
η X
η
S T
)
2
where η is the refractive index (1.33 for water and 1.36 for ethanol), K is the slope of integrated emission intensity against absorbance. The subscripts “ST” and “X” refer to the reference and experimental samples, respectively.

N, S-GQDs are prepared under UV irradiation by using catechol and o-phenylendiamine as raw materials. 1 mmol of o-phenylendiamine (0.108 g) and 1 mmol of catechol (0.11 g) were dissolved in 20 mL of DMSO. To make sure there was complete dissolution, ultrasonic vibration was performed for 5 min. The mixture was exposed to UV irradiation for 2 h. After cooling to room temperature, the solution was centrifuged at a rate of 12000 rpm for 20 min and the resulting supernatant was purified via a 0.22 μm filter to remove impurity and large particles. Then, the dark-brown product was dialyzed for 72 h (retained molecular weight: 1000 Da). Finally, the purified solution was frozen at −40 °C overnight and then lyophilized using a bench top freeze dryer (SCIENTZ-100F, Scientz) under condenser temperature −70 °C, pressure 10 Pa condition. The processing time was 48 h, and the N, S-GQDs solids were obtained for the further use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The concentration deployment of N, S-GQDs
ce:section.ce:para: 10 mg of N, S-GQDs powder was weighed with a balance and dissolved in 10 mL DMSO. The concentration of original N, S-GQDs was 1 mg/mL. Then different concentrations of N, S-GQDs solution were produced by diluting the synthesized N, S-GQDs solution. Detail concentrations of diluted N, S-GQDs were 0.02, 0.05, 0.08, 0.15, 0.27, 0.47 and 0.84 mg/mL, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of N, S-GQDs modified hydroxypropyl methyl cellulose
ce:section.ce:para: First, 0.5 g of HPMC was dissolved in 50 mL of deionized water, and the transparent HPMC solution was obtained by ultrasonic treatment and constant stirring. Then 3.36 mg of N, S-GQDs powder obtained by lyophilization was dissolved in 4 mL of HMPC solution, followed by ultrasound and stirring for 20 min until the powder was completely dissolved, and N, S-GQDs modified HPMC composite material was obtained, which was named as N, S-GQDs/HPMC.

N, S-GQDs are prepared under UV irradiation in DMSO solution for 2 h by using catechol as carbon source, o-phenylenediamine and DMSO as nitrogen and sulfur sources. Under the irradiation of 365 nm UV lamp, N, S-GQDs emit strong green fluorescence. The corresponding concentration-regulated multicolor of N, S-GQDs and the photothermal conversion process of N, S-GQDs/HPMC composites are shown in Scheme 1 . The surface morphology of the N, S-GQDs, measured by TEM, is shown in Fig. 1 (a). These N, S-GQDs are uniform in size with good dispersion. The lattice fringes are unambiguous and the corresponding lattice spacing is about 0.25 nm as shown in the inset. The particle size distribution of these N, S-GQDs is 1.0–4.5 nm and the average particle size is 2.61 nm, as shown in Fig. 1(b), which is obtained from statistical analysis for more than one hundred N, S-GQDs particles by using the Image J software. The AFM image of N, S-GQDs (Fig. 1(c)) shows the height is lower than 1.5 nm, about 2–3 graphene layers [33]. The Raman spectrum of the N, S-GQDs is recorded in Fig. 1(d). It was obvious that there are two peaks at 1380 cm−1 (D) and 1530 cm−1 (G). As an indicator of the defect density of crystalline structure, ID/IG in the N, S-GQDs is 0.78 [34,35]. In Fig. 1(e), the atomic percentages of C, N, O and S are 66.58%, 10.33%, 18.70% and 4.39% according to the results of full-scan XPS spectrum for N, S-GQDs, indicating the successful doping of N and S. The high-resolution spectrum of C 1s shown in Fig. 1(f) exhibits three peaks at 284.3 eV, 285.6 eV and 289.5 eV, which indicates the presence of CC/C–C, C–O/C–N and CO [36–38]. Four dominant peaks emerged from the XPS spectrum of N 1s are shown in Fig. 1(g), which are attributed to CN (398.3 eV), pyridinic N (389.9 eV), amino N (400.2 eV) and graphitic N (400.7 eV) [39–41], respectively. The O 1s spectrum shows three peaks at 530.9 eV, 532.2 eV and 533.0 eV, which can be attributed to the CO, C–O and C–OH/C–O–C bond (Fig. 1(h)) [42,43], respectively. The S 2p spectrum can be divided into peaks centered at 163.3 eV and 167.8 eV, indicating that S exists in two forms in N, S-GQDs (Fig. 1(i)). The peaks of 163.0 eV and 164.2 eV can be attributed to the 2p3/2 and 2p1/2 of thiophene-S due to the spin-orbit coupling. The latter peak can be divided into 168.5 eV and 168.9 eV, which are correspond with 2p3/2 and 2p1/2 of oxides-sulfur [44,45].

The optical property of as-prepared N, S-GQDs is identified by UV–vis and fluorescence spectra. The typical UV–vis absorption spectrum (black), photoluminescence (PL) excitation (cyan) and emission (green) spectra of the N, S-GQDs are shown in Fig. 2(a)
. It is found that typical π-π* transition of aromatic CC bonds and n-π* transition of CO/CN absorption bands are centered at around 287 nm and 447 nm, respectively [46]. The maximum emission wavelength is 530 nm with the excitation wavelength at 416 nm, while a shoulder peak is observed at about 440 nm, which may be related to the transition of electrons from the excited state to the ground state [47]. The N, S-GQDs solution is pale yellow under the sunlight and exhibits strong green fluorescence under irradiation with UV light in the inset of Fig. 2(a). The PL quantum yield is measured to be 32% for N, S-GQDs [48,49]. Meanwhile, the emission wavelength of the as-synthesized N, S-GQDs exhibits an excitation-independent behavior under various excitation wavelengths (Fig. 2(b)), which is attributed to the uniform size and surface state of the sp2 clusters [50]. The excitation-emission matrix of the N, S-GQDs solution is shown in Fig. 2(c), and a strong green emission peak is seen at the wavelength of 530 nm. The fluorescence stability of N, S-GQDs is further evaluated under different conditions. As shown in Fig. 2(d-e), the fluorescence intensity of as-prepared N, S-GQDs decreased slightly after storing at ultraviolet exposure for 26 h or room temperature for 24 days. Furthermore, the prepared N, S-GQDs show relatively stable PL intensity in NaCl solutions with different concentrations (0–100 mM, Fig. 2(f)). All these results demonstrate that N, S-GQDs have excellent resistance to photobleaching and antijamming capability, which can be attributed to the stable doping structure of N, S-GQDs [51].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Concentration-dependent emission behavior of N, S-GQDs
ce:section.ce:para: In the study of PL properties for N, S-GQDs, we found that the different concentration of N, S-GQDs solutions exhibit different color emissions from cyan to yellow under the UV light. Fig. 3 (a) shows normalized PL spectra of N, S-GQDs solutions with different concentrations, and the corresponding images under the visible light and UV light irradiation are shown in Fig. S1(a). With the increase of N, S-GQDs concentration from 0.02 mg/mL to 0.84 mg/mL, the maximum emission wavelength of the synthesized N, S-GQDs exhibits a distinct red-shift from 505 nm to 560 nm, as shown in Fig. S1(b). The corresponding Commission Internationale de L'Eclairage (CIE) coordinates of N, S-GQDs solutions with different concentrations are presented in Fig. 3(b). As per the result obtained from Fig. S1(c-i), the emission wavelengths of N, S-GQDs are irrelevant to the excitation wavelength at different concentrations, indicating that the red-shift of the emission wavelength is due to the change of N, S-GQDs solution state rather than excitation light wavelengths [52]. In order to understand the concentration-dependent properties of N, S-GQDs in detail, the UV–vis absorption spectra of N, S-GQDs are measured. As illustrated in Fig. 3(c), the absorption peak at 430 nm of N, S-GQDs gradually red shifts with the increase of solution concentration, indicating that N, S-GQDs have different energy gaps at different concentrations [53,54]. Therefore, the band gaps of N, S-GQDs are depicted in Fig. S2. As the concentration increases, the energy gap gradually declines. This indicates the effective energy transfer from dispersed N, S-GQDs to agglomerated ones. To verify the occurrence of the energy transfer process, fluorescence decay properties of N-GQDs with different concentrations are measured, as shown in Fig. 3(d). The excited-state lifetime of N, S-GQDs gradually decreased from 6.48 to 3.72 ns as the concentration increased. In view of the above results, a visual illustration for possible energy transfer diagram and emission processes of the N, S-GQDs is shown in Fig. 3(e). With the increase of concentration, abundant surface groups on N, S-GQDs facilitate the formation of hydrogen bonds [55], while the enhanced interaction in groups causes the self-assembly of aggregates. As a result, the N, S-GQDs polymerize with each other and exhibit a smaller band gap than a single GQD, which leads to a red-shift in the emission wavelength [55,56]. Besides, it is seen that the fluorescence spectra of different concentrations of N, S-GQDs exhibit a trend of first increasing and then decreasing in Fig. S3. The increased PL intensity, as the concentration increases from 0.02 mg/mL to 0.15 mg/mL, indicates a small aggregation of N, S-GQDs, resulting in formation of conjugated aromatic rings that oscillate with each other. Therefore, the nonradiative complexation process of N, S-GQDs decreases with the consumption of the excited state electrons [57]. Meanwhile, with the gradual increase of the concentration (from 0.15 mg/mL to 0.84 mg/mL), more aggregates are formed and the molecules of N, S-GQDs are in close contact. This enhances the nonradiative relaxation process of N, S-GQDs and shuts down the radiation channel, leading to the decrease of fluorescence intensity [58].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Light response and reversible light switch of N, S-GQDs/HPMC
ce:section.ce:para: To assess the photothermal properties of N, S-GQDs/HPMC composites, the N, S-GQDs/HPMC mixed solution is added to a quartz colorimetric dish and tighted. In order to approach the real lighting environment, the HPMC and N, S-GQDs/HPMC composites solutions are irradiated under a simulated solar with the light intensity of 100 mW/cm2. As shown in Fig. S4, the high light transmittance of pure HPMC solution remained almost constant under both light and no light conditions. By comparison, as shown in Fig. 4 (a), the transmissivity of N, S-GQDs/HPMC in the original state (0 min) mildly decreased in the range of 350 nm–650 nm due to the combination of N, S-GQDs with HPMC, and is almost identical to HPMC in the long wavelength range. Interestingly, the transmittance drops sharply to zero after 7 min illumination, and the state of N, S-GQDs/HPMC went from transparent to opaque. The result supports that our prepared N, S-GQDs/HPMC composites have photo-thermochromic properties. After that, the change of temperature accompanied by prolonged light duration is detected. As illustrated in Fig. 4(b), the temperature of HPMC solution gradually increases and remains steady at 40 °C after 7 min irradiation. And the transparency of HPMC is not change significantly after long-term illumination due to the lower temperature than the phase transition temperature of HPMC (45 °C) [13]. For N, S-GQDs/HPMC, the temperature increases sharply from the initial 20 °C–41 °C during 3 min irradiation, and reaches the stable-state (51 °C) after 7 min, thereby causes change of the transparency of N, S-GQDs/HMPC. The surface temperature of N, S-GQDs/HPMC gradually decreases upon the light source removed, while the state of N, S-GQDs/HPMC changes from opaque to transparent. As shown in Fig. 4(c), the surface temperatures of pure HPMC and N, S-GQDs/HPMC under different illumination times are characterized by the infrared thermal camera. Meanwhile, as shown in Fig. 4(d), N, S-GQDs/HPMC exhibits a stronger absorption in the UV–visible band than N, S-GQDs, which may be closely related to the stronger photothermal conversion ability of N, S-GQDs/HPMC.

Inspired by above results, it is proposed that the concentration of N, S-GQDs may lead to the significant influence on their light absorption ability and photothermal conversion capability. Therefore, the change of N, S-GQDs/HPMC state with different concentrations of N, S-GQDs at the same illumination time is studied. As shown in Fig. 4(e), the temperature of N, S-GQDs/HPMC is gradually elevated with the increasing content of N, S-GQDs, which is in consistent with the finding from Fig. S5. The postulated schematic mechanism is illustrated in Fig. 4(f). There are two major factors determining the photothermal conversion capability of N, S-GQDs/HPMC. One is the thermogenesis related to the micro-aggregated N, S-GQDs, and the other is the surface interaction between N, S-GQDs and HPMC. The structure of HPMC and N, S-GQDs/HPMC composites are characterized and contrasted in Fig. S6. From FT-IR and XPS results, we demonstrate that the surface interaction between N, S-GQDs and HPMC existed. Meanwhile, the introduction of nitrogen and sulfur can lead to strong influence on the coupling effect between GQDs and HPMC. As previously reported, the generating of micro-aggregated N, S-GQDs and the doping of nitrogen and sulfur can introduce new energy levels into electronic structures [59]. Since the nonradiative recombination of photoexcited electrons and holes from the conduction band (CB) and valence band (VB) would be extremely facilitated, the N, S-GQDs/HPMC composite material has resulted in greater photothermal conversion capability. This will enhance the conversion of light into thermal energy. In addition, the interaction between N, S-GQDs and HPMC is vital for the changes of energy levels and photoexcited carriers of N, S-GQDs [60]. Fig. S7 shows the PL emission spectra of N, S-GQDs and N, S-GQDs/HPMC, which are excited at 416 nm. Evidently, the PL intensity of N, S-GQDs/HPMC is weaker than that of N, S-GQDs, indicating the suppressed radiative recombination of photoexcited carriers. Moreover, the long chain entanglement of specially prepared N, S-GQDs/HPMC composites reduces the distance between the micro-aggregated N, S-GQDs, which results in greater aggregation and interaction of N, S-GQDs and enhances the photothermal conversion ability. Therefore, these results suggest that the photothermal conversion ability of N, S-GQDs/HPMC is due to the aggregation of N, S-GQDs enhancing light absorption and non-radiative recombination of photoexcited carriers, and the excellent performance of N, S-GQDs/HPMC composite for light modulation is also demonstrated.

To take into consideration of practicability, the outdoor tests are carried out to evaluate the optical switching property of N, S-GQDs/HPMC in response to weather conditions. Fig. S8 shows the digital photographs of N, S-GQDs/HPMC composites by ambient light irradiation stimuli. Noticeably, the composite shows changeable transparency with the variation of the light intensity. This fully demonstrates that the obtained N, S-GQDs/HPMC composites possess excellent solar modulation ability.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Light intensity sensing based on the temperature variation of N, S-GQDs/HPMC
ce:section.ce:para: Based on the excellent photothermal conversion capability of N, S-GQDs/HPMC composites, a convenient light intensity sensing platform is designed. Fig. 5 (a) depicts the fabrication of the planar microcavity device. The device is composed of two mirrors and N, S-GQDs/HPMC in a sandwich structure. Fig. S9 presents a photograph of the microcavity device and its structure diagram. The daylight images of the device show a uniform distribution of N, S-GQDs/HPMC and a transition to opaque state under the light irradiation. Subsequently, we measure the temperature of N, S-GQDs/HPMC solution in the microcavity under different lighting conditions. As shown in Fig. 5(b), when the light intensity changes from 5 to 150 mW/cm2, the temperature of N, S-GQDs/HPMC is continuously increased. And the microcavity also shows obvious changes of the transparency state and surface temperature. Furthermore, according to Fig. 5(c), as the light intensity increases, the red line represents the good linear dependence between temperature values and light intensity, and the correlation coefficient (R2) is 0.984. Inversely, when the light intensity decreases, the changes of temperature values also show a good linear relationship with the R2 = 0.983. Fig. 5(d) demonstrates the stability and repeatability of N, S-GQDs/HPMC solution by light on and light off experiments. It shows that the temperature gradually increases as the light intensity changes from 5 to 150 mW/cm2, then recovers to its initial value when light intensity comes back to 5 mW/cm2. Moreover, this process can be repeated many times and the temperature is almost constant, which means stable and wide-range sensing ability of N, S-GQDs/HPMC for light intensity sensing. Therefore, the N, S-GQDs/HPMC is suitable as a light intensity sensing material with high sensitivity and repeatability. Meanwhile, the integrated microcavity with N, S-GQDs/HPMC also provides a new idea for portable detection of external light intensity.

In summary, we have synthesized N, S-GQDs by using o-phenylendiamine and catechol as raw materials under UV irradiation in DMSO solution for 2 h. The resultant N, S-GQDs solution can emit intense green fluorescence under 365 nm UV light and the emission wavelength has obvious red-shift with the increasing concentration of N, S-GQDs. Moreover, a photothermal conversion film of N, S-GQDs/HPMC composite is developed by incorporating N, S-GQDs into hydroxypropyl methyl cellulose. The obtained N, S-GQDs/HPMC composite material exhibits photothermal conversion capability and reversible light modulation performance, and the temperature of N, S-GQDs/HPMC can reach 51 °C after 7 min irradiation, which can realize the state transition from transparent to opaque. Besides, the N, S-GQDs/HPMC exhibits remarkable photoresponsive in a range of 5–150 mW/cm2 with good linearity, reversibility, and recoverability. Therefore, a convenient planar microcavity device has been realized based on the N, S-GQDs/HPMC composite film with photothermal effect. Our work can provide material strategies and device architectures for exploiting GQDs for the applications in photothermal transmission techniques.

Siyuan Tang: analyzed experimental results, Writing – original draft. Da Chen: conducted the experiments, revised the manuscript, Funding acquisition. Changxing Wang: involved in analyzing experimental results. Yongsheng Yang: assisted in analyzing experimental results and software data processing. Xiameng Li: assisted in analyzing experimental results and software data processing. Tingting Li: Investigation, Formal analysis. Xiaohong Zhang: Writing – review & editing, All authors read and contributed to the manuscript.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by projects from National Natural Science Foundation of China under Grant (Nos. 11704204, 61604084, and 51802337). K. C. Wong Magna Fund in Ningbo University. The authors declare no competing ﬁnancial interest.

ce:section.ce:para:
